text: The video shows a direct inguinal hernia located medial to the inferior epigastric vessels, clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon creates a preperitoneal flap to fully expose the hernia space, initiating dissection in a sweeping motion from top to bottom.

The surgeon identifies the hernia contents composed of preperitoneal adipose tissue protruding through the direct hernia space. The preperitoneal sac is dissected from the adipose tissue, and the contents are reduced back into the abdominal cavity.

Cooper's ligament is exposed during the dissection, followed by evaluation of the indirect space. A cord lipoma is carefully dissected from the vas deferens and testicular vessels, with no indirect hernia observed.
The surgeon deploys a surgical mesh into the preperitoneal space after exposing all potential hernia spaces and completing the dissection.

This is a self-fixating mesh and therefore will not require sutures or tacks to remain in its position. This has drastically reduced the postoperative pain reported by patients. In my experience, most patients will not fill their pain prescriptions after this operation.
The surgeon positions the self-fixating mesh to cover all potential hernia spaces. The preperitoneal space is then sutured closed, marking the completion of the operation.
